characters.description: Avar naib who has defected to the Russians. Now lodged under guard in Major Petrov's fort awaiting action, he charms Butler and Marya Dmitrievna while worrying about his captive family.
Young Russian officer recently exchanged from the Guards to the Caucasus to escape gambling debts. Romantic about war and the mountaineer life, befriends Hadji Murad during his fort stay.
Hard-drinking Russian fort commander who lives with Marya Dmitrievna as common-law wife. Receives Hadji Murad under orders and hosts him, treating him with gruff pragmatism.
Former surgeon's-orderly daughter, now Major Petrov's faithful freckled common-law wife. Warm, simple-hearted, takes a particular liking to Hadji Murad and defends his character.
Company mascot dog that runs ahead of Butler's returning column with its tail curled up like a commander.
Imam of Chechnya and Dagestan, returning to Vedeno after a disputed battle. Holds Hadji Murad's family hostage and threatens Yusuf with blinding to force Hadji Murad's return.
Chechen villager who had sheltered Hadji Murad. Returns to find his saklya destroyed and his son bayoneted to death by Russian soldiers.
Hadji Murad's grey-haired, fiery-eyed old mother held with the family at Vedeno. Refuses even to watch Shamil's triumphal entry, having always hated him.
Hadji Murad's handsome eighteen-year-old son imprisoned in a seven-foot pit at Vedeno. Admires Shamil out of defiance to his father; forced to write a coercive letter, then tries to stab himself.
Shamil's officer in whose saklya Hadji Murad's women and children are held under guard at Vedeno.
Shamil's tall, white-bearded father-in-law, teacher and chief advisor. Drafts the proclamation against surrender and counsels on Hadji Murad's case.
Shamil's black-eyed eighteen-year-old favorite wife. Sulks in Zeidat's room because Shamil gave silk to Zeidat instead of her, refusing to come to him.
Shamil's dark, sharp-nosed, disagreeable eldest wife whom he does not love. Serves him dinner and has been favored with silk over the younger Aminal.
One of the two murids Hadji Murad brought with him to the fort. Sworn brother and tenor singer who performs mountain songs for Butler.
The second of Hadji Murad's murids at the fort. Serves Butler attentively, hands Hadji Murad his burka and sword on departure.
Lively Russian officer meeting Hadji Murad for the first time at his farewell gathering. Questions him about Tiflis and later calls him a rogue.
Small Kumukh prince from Tash-Kichu with a blood-feud against Hadji Murad. Ambushes him at the major's porch with a pistol; misses and is disarmed.
Major Petrov's orderly who appears and is sent to open the front door when Hadji Murad arrives.
Tsar of Russia, referenced as the issuer of the January 1852 command that triggers the raid into Chechnya opening chapter 16.

fusion_hints.reason: The two murids accompanying Hadji Murad into the fort function as an undifferentiated loyal-retainer pair. Khanefi sings and is the sworn brother; Eldar serves. A screenplay can fuse their functions into one composite murid without losing plot.

Shamil's two named wives serve a single dramatic function: illustrating his domestic frustration after the battle. Can be collapsed into one wife who both serves dinner and sulks, preserving the scene beat.
Bondarenko is a one-line orderly cameo who only opens a door. Easily cut entirely or merged with another servant/orderly role; not a fusion with Eldar per se but absorbable into the household.

Ibrahim Raschid is a pure setting-label (the officer whose saklya holds the family). His function can be absorbed into Jemal Eddin or an unnamed guard-officer in Shamil's court without narrative loss.

Petrovsky is a one-scene interlocutor at the farewell party who voices suspicion of Hadji Murad. His lines could be redistributed to Butler or the Major; he exists only to provide a dissenting opinion.

Both are grief-stricken parental figures mourning Russian-caused destruction within the same narrative beat (chapter 17 / chapter 19 framing). A film could fuse the destroyed-village grief with the mother's grief into one representative lament.
